thesis: Rockwell Automation is the largest pure-play industrial-automation company in the world and a prime beneficiary of factory reshoring, digital transformation, and the automation of AI-era infrastructure (data centers, semiconductor fabs, and logistics). After a soft FY2025 (revenue +1% to $8.34B as customers digested prior overordering), momentum has re-accelerated: the most recent quarter delivered ~12% revenue growth and an EPS beat, management raised FY2026 adjusted EPS guidance to $12.50–$13.10, and margin expansion continues across all three segments. The company pairs a wide moat (installed base, Logix control ecosystem, switching costs) with a $1B buyback and a growing software and recurring-revenue mix. The catch is valuation: at ~$462 the stock trades near 48x trailing and ~33x forward earnings — well above its historical range and near 52-week highs, with the ~$471 analyst consensus implying only low-single-digit upside. Excellent franchise, full price — hence HOLD.

drivers: Data-center and semiconductor automation demand accelerates, adding a durable secular growth layer on top of Rockwell's traditional cyclical end markets
FY2026 adjusted EPS lands at or above the top of the $12.50–$13.10 guide and FY2027 approaches $15 as reshoring and digital-transformation capex broadens
Software & Control and recurring/annual-recurring revenue keep compounding at 30%+ segment margins, structurally lifting the blended margin and multiple
Order momentum and book-to-bill stay above 1.0 as manufacturers scale AI, MES, and cybersecurity (FactoryTalk, Plex, SecureOT)
Continued margin expansion (segment margin already 20.7%, +360 bps YoY) plus the $1B buyback drive double-digit EPS growth and the premium multiple holds

base_case.assumptions: Rockwell delivers its raised FY2026 guide (adjusted EPS $12.50–$13.10) on mid-to-high-single-digit organic growth, with continued but moderating margin expansion. Data-center, semiconductor, and logistics automation offset softer traditional discrete and process markets. The stock stays a premium industrial at ~33–38x forward earnings and drifts modestly higher toward the ~$471 consensus as the cyclical recovery is confirmed rather than re-rated sharply.

risks: The industrial capex cycle rolls over and organic growth decelerates, echoing the FY2024–2025 destocking downturn
The premium ~33x forward / ~48x trailing multiple compresses toward Rockwell's historical mid-20s as growth slows
Data-center and semiconductor automation demand proves lumpier or lower-margin than hoped and does not offset core-market weakness
Tariffs, supply-chain costs, and pricing give-back pressure the hard-won margin expansion
A broad industrials de-rating or recession hits a name trading near 52-week highs and above the analyst target

catalysts.event: Q3 FY2026 earnings — organic growth, orders/book-to-bill, and any further FY2026 guidance raise
Execution against raised FY2026 adjusted EPS guidance of $12.50–$13.10 and continued segment-margin expansion
Data-center, semiconductor-fab, and logistics automation order momentum as AI-era infrastructure is built out
Q4 and full-year FY2026 results with initial FY2027 guidance
Execution of the $1B share-repurchase authorization and growth in software / annual recurring revenue (FactoryTalk, Plex)

key_risks: Full valuation: ~48x trailing and ~33x forward earnings, above the historical mid-20s range and near 52-week highs, with consensus target only ~2% above the price
Cyclicality: industrial automation capex is tied to manufacturing cycles and can swing sharply, as the FY2024–2025 destocking downturn showed
Reliance on data-center and semiconductor demand that may prove lumpy, project-timing dependent, or lower-margin than the core business
Tariff, input-cost, and pricing pressures that could reverse recent margin gains
Competition from Siemens, Schneider Electric, Emerson, ABB, and Honeywell across automation, software, and process end markets
Macro/recession sensitivity given a high beta (~1.5) and premium multiple
